items.reality: They return lazy iterators that yield on demand; wrap in list() only when you truly need all items at once.
items.example: `chain(a, b)` returns a `chain` object — `print(chain(a,b))` shows `<itertools.chain at 0x...>`. To see items, iterate or `list(...)` it. The laziness is the whole point — no large intermediate copies.

items.reality: It only helps when the same arguments recur and the function is pure; for unique inputs it just adds overhead and memory.
items.example: Caching `embed(unique_doc_text)` where every doc differs → 100% misses + a growing dict eating RAM. Useful only when the function is called repeatedly with the same arguments.

items.myth: reduce is the Pythonic way to sum or combine.
items.reality: Prefer sum(), min()/max(), or a comprehension/loop for clarity; reduce is reserved for genuinely custom folds.
items.example: Sum a list? `sum(xs)` (clear, fast). Not `reduce(operator.add, xs, 0)` (slower, opaque). Reserve `reduce` for things without a built-in shortcut (e.g. merging dicts).
